• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Statistical Case Study: Predicting Post-Graduation Earnings

Tim Sheppel

03/07/2026

Section 0: Setup

library(car)
library(psych)
library(corrplot)
library(ggplot2)
library(Hmisc)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

Section 1: Data Loading and Summary

college_data <- read.csv("collegescorecard.csv")
summary(college_data)

View(college_data)
dim(college_data)
## [1] 1326   19

Section 2: Research Question

Background

This study is focused on using linear regression to analyze the relationship between college tuition and earnings. Our dependent variable (outcome) is earn10yr — the median earnings of students 10 years after entering the institution.

Variable Description
unitid Unique institution identifier
instname Institution name
city City
state State
control 1 = public, 2 = private nonprofit
adm_rate Admission rate (0-100)
sat_avg Average SAT score (~850-1540)
enrollment Total student enrollment
pctpell Percent Pell grant recipients (0-100, proxy for socioeconomic status)
pctfloan Percent receiving federal loans (0-100)
avg_net_price Average net price in dollars
grad_rate 6-year graduation rate (0-100) — an interesting predictor to consider!
retention_rate First-year retention rate (0-100)
earn10yr Median earnings 10 years after entry (DV)
pct_edu_program Percent education programs
avg_facsalary Average faculty monthly salary
ft_faculty Percent full-time faculty (0-100)
instr_expend Instructional expenditure per FTE student
pell_cat 1 = low Pell (<=33%), 2 = medium (33-66%), 3 = high (>66%)

2.1 Research Question

Among four-year institutions, to what extent do measures of access (Pell share, financial aid receipt), selectivity (admission rate), scale (enrollment), and sector (public vs. private) explain variation in median earnings 10 years after entry, and which factors matter most for policy?

2.2 Variables

  1. Percentage of Students Receiving Pell Grants(pctpell): We include this variable because it captures the share of students from lower-income backgrounds at an institution. We expect a positive relationship with earnings: schools that enroll more Pell recipients may have stronger support systems and completion-focused policies, and aid may enable degree completion, which is associated with higher earnings.
  2. Pell Grant Category(pell_cat): We include Pell grant category (low, moderate, or high income) to distinguish the intensity of need-based aid at the institution. This helps control for the socioeconomic composition of the student body and may capture different labor-market pathways or selection effects that could influence post-graduation earnings.
  3. Percentage of Students Receiving Financial Aid(pctfloan): We include this because it reflects how accessible the institution is to students who need aid to attend. Higher financial aid receipt is often associated with degree completion and persistence; we include it to capture this channel and expect a positive relationship with earnings.
  4. Admission Rate(adm_rate): We include admission rate as a measure of institutional selectivity (exclusivity). More selective schools (lower admission rates) tend to have stronger signaling value to employers, better placement networks, and higher-achieving peers, so we expect a negative relationship between admission rate and earnings.
  5. Total Enrollment(enrollment): We include total enrollment to evaluate scale of institutional student bodies. Smaller, more exclusive institutions might be more accessible to students with more opportunities. We expect a negative relationship with earnings, as smaller or more selective enrollments may be associated with stronger outcomes.
  6. Institutional Type(control): We include public vs. private designation because institutional type is associated with resources, prestige, and labor-market pipelines. Private institutions often have different alumni networks and employer relationships, so we include it to control for or estimate this dimension of the earnings relationship.

3. Data Cleaning

3.2 Missing Values

colSums(is.na(college_data))
##          unitid        instname            city           state         control 
##               0               0               0               0               0 
##        adm_rate         sat_avg      enrollment         pctpell        pctfloan 
##               0               0               0               0               0 
##   avg_net_price       grad_rate  retention_rate        earn10yr pct_edu_program 
##               0               0               0               0               0 
##   avg_facsalary      ft_faculty    instr_expend        pell_cat 
##               0               0               0               0
# Handle missing values by removing rows with any missing values
college_data <- na.omit(college_data)
# Check descriptives again after removing missing data
describe(college_data)

3.3 Check for Duplicate Values

college_data$dup_unitid <- duplicated(college_data$unitid)
table(college_data$dup_unitid)
## 
## FALSE 
##  1326

3.4 Summary Statistics

summary(college_data)
##      unitid         instname             city              state          
##  Min.   :100654   Length:1326        Length:1326        Length:1326       
##  1st Qu.:152539   Class :character   Class :character   Class :character  
##  Median :183067   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character  
##  Mean   :182728                                                           
##  3rd Qu.:214650                                                           
##  Max.   :448840                                                           
##     control         adm_rate         sat_avg       enrollment

##  1st Qu.:1.000   1st Qu.: 53.81   1st Qu.: 976   1st Qu.: 1348

##  retention_rate      earn10yr      pct_edu_program  avg_facsalary  
##  Min.   :  0.00   Min.   : 17600   Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 1476  
##  1st Qu.: 68.37   1st Qu.: 36500   1st Qu.: 0.530   1st Qu.: 6112  
##  Median : 75.98   Median : 41400   Median : 5.570   Median : 7199  
##  Mean   : 75.75   Mean   : 42939   Mean   : 7.147   Mean   : 7526  
##  3rd Qu.: 83.61   3rd Qu.: 47475   3rd Qu.:11.227   3rd Qu.: 8509  
##  Max.   :100.00   Max.   :116400   Max.   :64.970   Max.   :19862  
##    ft_faculty      instr_expend       pell_cat     dup_unitid     
##  Min.   :  4.03   Min.   :  1889   Min.   :1.000   Mode :logical  
##  1st Qu.: 53.15   1st Qu.:  6514   1st Qu.:1.000   FALSE:1326     
##  Median : 71.93   Median :  8243   Median :2.000                  
##  Mean   : 70.82   Mean   : 10135   Mean   :1.619                  
##  3rd Qu.: 94.44   3rd Qu.: 10790   3rd Qu.:2.000                  
##  Max.   :100.00   Max.   :106214   Max.   :3.000

There were no missing values or duplicate values found in the data.  

3.5 Extension 1: Dummy Coding

# Create a new variable is_public that is 1 if control is (Public) and 0 if control is (Private Nonprofit)
college_data$is_public <- ifelse(college_data$control == 1, 1, 0)
# Verify the new variable
table(college_data$control, college_data$is_public)
##    
##       0   1
##   1   0 502
##   2 824   0
# Create dummy variables for each Pell category (1 = Low ≤33%, 2 = Medium 33–66%, 3 = High >66%)
college_data$pell_cat_low <- ifelse(college_data$pell_cat == 1, 1, 0)
college_data$pell_cat_medium <- ifelse(college_data$pell_cat == 2, 1, 0)
college_data$pell_cat_high <- ifelse(college_data$pell_cat == 3, 1, 0)

# Verify the dummy variables match the original
table(college_data$pell_cat, college_data$pell_cat_low, college_data$pell_cat_medium, college_data$pell_cat_high)
## , ,  = 0,  = 0
## 
##    
##       0   1
##   1   0 549
##   2   0   0
##   3   0   0
## 
## , ,  = 1,  = 0
## 
##    
##       0   1
##   1   0   0
##   2 733   0
##   3   0   0
## 
## , ,  = 0,  = 1
## 
##    
##       0   1
##   1   0   0
##   2   0   0
##   3  44   0
## 
## , ,  = 1,  = 1
## 
##    
##       0   1
##   1   0   0
##   2   0   0
##   3   0   0
# Check that new dummy columns exist and contain expected values
head(college_data) 
##   unitid                            instname       city state control adm_rate
## 1 100654            Alabama A & M University     Normal    AL       1    89.89
## 2 100663 University of Alabama at Birmingham Birmingham    AL       1    86.73
## 3 100706 University of Alabama in Huntsville Huntsville    AL       1    80.62
## 4 100724            Alabama State University Montgomery    AL       1    51.25
## 5 100751           The University of Alabama Tuscaloosa    AL       1    56.55
## 6 100830     Auburn University at Montgomery Montgomery    AL       1    83.71
##   sat_avg enrollment pctpell pctfloan avg_net_price grad_rate retention_rate
## 1     823       4051   71.15    82.04         13415     29.14          63.14
## 2    1146      11200   35.05    53.97         14805     53.77          80.16
## 3    1180       5525   32.81    47.28         17520     48.35          80.98
## 4     830       5354   82.65    87.35         11936     25.17          62.19
## 5    1171      28692   21.07    41.48         20916     66.65          87.00
## 6     970       4322   40.06    64.76         11915     27.05          63.21
##   earn10yr pct_edu_program avg_facsalary ft_faculty instr_expend pell_cat
## 1    31400           14.90          7079      88.56         7459        3
## 2    40300            8.62         10170      91.06        17208        2
## 3    46600            1.73          9341      65.55         9352        1
## 4    27800           21.50          6557      66.41         7393        3
## 5    42400            8.40          9605      71.09         9817        1
## 6    34800           14.06          7173      92.62         6817        2
##   dup_unitid is_public pell_cat_low pell_cat_medium pell_cat_high
## 1      FALSE         1            0               0             1
## 2      FALSE         1            0               1             0
## 3      FALSE         1            1               0             0
## 4      FALSE         1            0               0             1
## 5      FALSE         1            1               0             0
## 6      FALSE         1            0               1             0

3.6 Initial Scatterplots

plot(college_data$pctpell, college_data$earn10yr,
main="Median Earnings vs. % Pell Grant Recipients",
xlab="% Pell Grant Recipients",
ylab="Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry")

plot(college_data$pctfloan, college_data$earn10yr,
main="Median Earnings vs. % Financial Aid Recipients",
xlab="% Financial Aid Recipients",
ylab="Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry")

plot(college_data$adm_rate, college_data$earn10yr,
main="Median Earnings vs. Admission Rate",
xlab="Admission Rate",
ylab="Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry")

plot(college_data$enrollment, college_data$earn10yr,
main="Median Earnings vs. Total Enrollment",
xlab="Total Enrollment",
ylab="Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry")

It appears that there is a negative relationship between median earnings and the percentage of students receiving Pell grants, financial aid, and possibly admission rate. The relationship between median earnings and total enrollment is not as clear.  

3.7 Initial Categorical Boxplots

boxplot(earn10yr ~ is_public, data = college_data,
        main = "Median Earnings vs. Institutional Type",
        xlab = "Institutional Type (1 = Public, 0 = Private Nonprofit)",
        ylab = "Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry",
        names = c("Private Nonprofit", "Public"))

There appears to be a small negative relationship between median earnings and the Pell grant category.  

college_data$pell_group <- with(college_data,
  ifelse(pell_cat_low == 1, "Low",
  ifelse(pell_cat_medium == 1, "Medium",
  ifelse(pell_cat_high == 1, "High", NA))))
college_data$pell_group <- factor(college_data$pell_group, levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))

boxplot(earn10yr ~ pell_group, data = college_data,
        main = "Median Earnings by Pell Category",
        xlab = "Pell Category", ylab = "Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry")

The change in median earnings across the three Pell grant categories is evident.   —

Section 4: Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Correlation Analysis

# Select only numeric columns for correlation
numeric_cols <- college_data[, sapply(college_data, is.numeric)]
corr_results <- rcorr(as.matrix(numeric_cols))
# View correlation coefficients (rounded to 3 decimal places)
round(corr_results$r, 3)
##                 unitid control adm_rate sat_avg enrollment pctpell pctfloan
## unitid           1.000  -0.042    0.070  -0.048     -0.075  -0.052    0.035
## control         -0.042   1.000   -0.152   0.140     -0.553  -0.087    0.272
## adm_rate         0.070  -0.152    1.000  -0.362     -0.032   0.079    0.269
## sat_avg         -0.048   0.140   -0.362   1.000      0.224  -0.742   -0.526
## enrollment      -0.075  -0.553   -0.032   0.224      1.000  -0.137   -0.376
## pctpell         -0.052  -0.087    0.079  -0.742     -0.137   1.000    0.504
## pctfloan         0.035   0.272    0.269  -0.526     -0.376   0.504    1.000
## avg_net_price   -0.042   0.614   -0.088   0.392     -0.231  -0.457    0.122
## grad_rate        0.014   0.229   -0.295   0.832      0.173  -0.719   -0.339
## retention_rate  -0.078   0.012   -0.258   0.766      0.302  -0.628   -0.420
## earn10yr         0.002   0.081   -0.269   0.638      0.178  -0.569   -0.343
## pct_edu_program -0.013   0.004    0.165  -0.266     -0.133   0.171    0.207
## avg_facsalary   -0.025  -0.152   -0.326   0.662      0.412  -0.515   -0.501
## ft_faculty       0.028  -0.075   -0.039   0.136      0.053  -0.082   -0.063
## instr_expend    -0.050   0.122   -0.452   0.648      0.094  -0.410   -0.425
## pell_cat        -0.019  -0.088    0.053  -0.636     -0.112   0.851    0.428
## is_public        0.042  -1.000    0.152  -0.140      0.553   0.087   -0.272
## pell_cat_low     0.015   0.104   -0.097   0.601      0.093  -0.765   -0.385

round(corr_results$P, 4)

## pell_cat_medium        0.0000     0.0002       0.0000   0.0000    0.0001
## pell_cat_high          0.0000     0.4756       0.0048   0.0000    0.6006
##                 pell_cat_low pell_cat_medium pell_cat_high
## unitid                0.5879          0.7542        0.5347
## control               0.0002          0.0001        0.6006
## adm_rate              0.0004          0.0000        0.0001
## sat_avg               0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## enrollment            0.0007          0.0276        0.0014
## pctpell               0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## pctfloan              0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## avg_net_price         0.0000          0.0000        0.0001
## grad_rate             0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## retention_rate        0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## earn10yr              0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## pct_edu_program       0.0000          0.0000        0.0808
## avg_facsalary         0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## ft_faculty            0.0006          0.0002        0.4756
## instr_expend          0.0000          0.0000        0.0048
## pell_cat              0.0000          0.0000        0.0000
## is_public             0.0002          0.0001        0.6006
## pell_cat_low              NA          0.0000        0.0000
## pell_cat_medium       0.0000              NA        0.0000
## pell_cat_high         0.0000          0.0000            NA
write.csv(round(corr_results$r, 3), file = "Correlation Matrix College Scorecard.csv")

4.2 Visualization: Correlation Matrix

corrplot(corr_results$r, method = "color", type = "upper",
         tl.cex = 0.7, tl.col = "black",
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.5,
         title = "Correlation Matrix - College Scorecard",
         mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

4.3 Density Plot: Median Earnings by Pell Grant Category

college_data$pell_cat_label <- factor(college_data$pell_cat,
labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))

ggplot(college_data, aes(x = earn10yr, fill = as.factor(pell_cat_label))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Median Earnings Distribution by Pell Grant Category",
       x = "Median Earnings 10 Years After Entry",
       y = "Density",
       fill = "Pell Grant Category")

Average SAT score (0.638), First-year retention rate (0.615), Six-year graduation rate (0.633), First-year retention rate (0.615), Average faculty monthly salary (0.690), Percent receiving Pell grants (-0.569), Instructional expenditure per FTE student (0.513), and Pell grant category (-0.499) have the strongest correlations with median earnings.

 

Section 5: Bivariate Regression Analysis

5.1 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Percentage of Students Receiving Pell Grants

model_pell <- lm(earn10yr ~ pctpell, data = college_data)
summary(model_pell)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ pctpell, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -19880  -5118  -1274   3891  70878 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value            Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 57669.97     629.02   91.68 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## pctpell      -403.73      16.06  -25.14 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 8339 on 1324 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3232, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3227 
## F-statistic: 632.3 on 1 and 1324 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

The slope coefficient is -403.73, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.3232. This means that for every 1% increase in the percentage of students receiving Pell grants, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to decrease by $403.73.

 

Section 6: Regression Diagnostics – Bivariate Regression

6.1 Histogram of Standardized Residuals

hist(scale(model_pell$residuals),
     main = "Histogram of Standardized Residuals",
     xlab = "Standardized Residuals",
     col = "lightblue",
     breaks = 20)

This histogram shows that the standardized residuals are normally distributed. ## 6.2 Diagnostic Plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_pell)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
  • Residuals vs Fitted: Points appear to be randomly scattered around 0. Nonlinearity is not evident.
  • Normal Q-Q: Points appear to fall along the diagonal line and taper off towards the tail end. This deviation does not indicate non-normal residuals.
  • Scale-Location: The spread of residuals appears to be roughly constant across fitted values. Heteroscedasticity is not evident.
  • Residuals vs Leverage: There are no points with high leverage AND large residuals (upper or lower right corners). There are no influential observations.

6.3 Standardized Coefficients

lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctpell), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctpell), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)          scale(pctpell)  
## -0.0000000000000001943  -0.5685046662959296615
lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pell_cat), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pell_cat), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##           (Intercept)        scale(pell_cat)  
## -0.000000000000002088  -0.498976227193217325
lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctfloan), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctfloan), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)         scale(pctfloan)  
## -0.0000000000000002368  -0.3429332815211152408
lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(adm_rate), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(adm_rate), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)         scale(adm_rate)  
## -0.0000000000000003061  -0.2687809287199865449
lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(enrollment), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(enrollment), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)       scale(enrollment)  
## -0.0000000000000002095   0.1782549759981875148
lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(control), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(control), data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)          scale(control)  
## -0.0000000000000001407   0.0806537491864878359

The standardized coefficients are:

  • Percentage of Students Receiving Pell Grants: -0.569
  • Pell Grant Category: -0.499
  • Percentage of Students Receiving Financial Aid: -0.342
  • Admission Rate: 0.269
  • Total Enrollment: 0.178
  • Institutional Type: 0.081

6.4 Standardized Residuals – Identifying Outliers

model_pell$standardized.residuals <- rstandard(model_pell)
model_pell$large_residual <- abs(model_pell$standardized.residuals) > 2

# How many potential outliers?
sum(model_pell$large_residual)
## [1] 52

There are 52 potential outliers in the (model_pell) regression with a standardized residual greater than 2.

6.5 Durbin-Watson Test

We are doing a Durbin-Watson test to check for autocorrelation in the residuals to see if errors are independent of each other.

dwt(model_pell)
##  lag Autocorrelation D-W Statistic p-value
##    1       0.1877696      1.624395       0
##  Alternative hypothesis: rho != 0

The Durbin-Watson test statistic is 1.62, which is close to 2, which indicates no autocorrelation in the residuals.

 

Section 7: Sequential Model Building

7.2 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Pell Grant Category

model_pell_cat <- lm(earn10yr ~ pell_cat_low + pell_cat_medium + pell_cat_high, data = college_data)
summary(model_pell_cat)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ pell_cat_low + pell_cat_medium + pell_cat_high, 
##     data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -24021  -5453  -1321   4085  67779 
## 
## Coefficients: (1 not defined because of singularities)
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value             Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        30114       1325  22.731 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## pell_cat_low       18507       1377  13.442 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## pell_cat_medium     9339       1364   6.847      0.0000000000115 ***
## pell_cat_high         NA         NA      NA                   NA    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 8787 on 1323 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.249,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.2478 
## F-statistic: 219.3 on 2 and 1323 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

As the baseline, the intercept for pell_cat_high is 30114, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.249. This means for a student that falls in the highest Pell grant category (>66%) the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to be $30,114.

The coefficient for pell_cat_medium is 9339, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.249. This means for a student that falls in the middle Pell grant category (33-66%), the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to be $9,339 higher than the estimate for the highest Pell Grant category, resulting in a total estimate of $39,453.

The coefficient for pell_cat_low is 18507, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.249. This means for a student that falls in the lowest Pell grant category (0-33%), the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to be $18,507 higher than the estimate for the highest Pell Grant category, resulting in a total estimate of $48,621.

7.3 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Percentage of Students Receiving Financial Aid

model_financial_aid <- lm(earn10yr ~ pctfloan, data = college_data)
summary(model_financial_aid)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ pctfloan, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -37373  -5983  -1015   4376  77278 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value            Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  55657.3      992.5   56.08 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## pctfloan      -211.2       15.9  -13.28 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 9522 on 1324 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1176, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1169 
## F-statistic: 176.5 on 1 and 1324 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

The slope coefficient is -211.2, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.1176. This means that for every 1% increase in the percentage of students receiving financial aid, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to decrease by $211.2.

7.4 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Admission Rate

model_adm_rate <- lm(earn10yr ~ adm_rate, data = college_data)
summary(model_adm_rate)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ adm_rate, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -27398  -5727   -970   5178  77121 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value            Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  52324.5      962.5   54.37 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## adm_rate      -146.2       14.4  -10.15 <0.0000000000000002 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 9763 on 1324 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.07224,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.07154 
## F-statistic: 103.1 on 1 and 1324 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

The slope coefficient is -146.2, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.0722. This means that for every 1% increase in admission rate, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to decrease by $146.2.

7.5 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Total Enrollment

model_enrollment <- lm(earn10yr ~ enrollment, data = college_data)
summary(model_enrollment)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ enrollment, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -24050  -6344  -1856   4159  73907 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                Estimate  Std. Error t value             Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 41535.13853   346.94185 119.718 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## enrollment      0.25161     0.03817   6.592      0.0000000000626 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 9974 on 1324 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.03177,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.03104 
## F-statistic: 43.45 on 1 and 1324 DF,  p-value: 0.00000000006263

The slope coefficient is 0.2516, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.03177. This means that for each additional enrollment in total enrollment, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to increase by $0.2516.

7.6 Regression Analysis: Median Earnings vs. Institutional Type

model_control <- lm(earn10yr ~ is_public, data = college_data)
summary(model_control)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ is_public, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -25976  -6455  -1584   4608  72824 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value             Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)    43577        352 123.810 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## is_public      -1684        572  -2.944              0.00329 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 10100 on 1324 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.006505,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.005755 
## F-statistic: 8.669 on 1 and 1324 DF,  p-value: 0.003293

The intercept for private institutions is 43577, which is not statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.0065. This means that for a student that graduated from a private institution, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to be $43,577.

The coefficient for public institutions is -1684, which is statistically significant at the 0.001 level, and with an R-squared of 0.0065. This means that for a student that graduated from a public institution, the median earnings 10 years after entry is expected to be $1684 lower than the estimate for the students that graduated from a private institution, resulting in a total estimate of $41,893.

7.7 Final Model

model_final <- lm(earn10yr ~ pctpell + pell_cat_high + pell_cat_medium + pctfloan + adm_rate + enrollment + is_public, data = college_data)
summary(model_final)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = earn10yr ~ pctpell + pell_cat_high + pell_cat_medium + 
##     pctfloan + adm_rate + enrollment + is_public, data = college_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -18248  -4788  -1063   3381  72946 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate  Std. Error t value             Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     62551.20365  1203.98865  51.953 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## pctpell          -349.41734    31.72666 -11.013 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## pell_cat_high    -685.55815  2017.68555  -0.340              0.73408    
## pell_cat_medium -1270.57353   757.78268  -1.677              0.09384 .  
## pctfloan           11.21493    17.83952   0.629              0.52968    
## adm_rate         -114.97508    12.86613  -8.936 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
## enrollment          0.20901     0.03823   5.467         0.0000000547 ***
## is_public       -1584.43997   584.98811  -2.708              0.00685 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 7938 on 1318 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3895, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3863 
## F-statistic: 120.1 on 7 and 1318 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

The Adjusted R-squared for the final model is 0.3863, which is 0.0631 higher than the R-squared for the model_pell regression of 0.3232. Pell grant category (pell_cat) and percentage of students receiving financial aid (pctfloan) have lost statistical significance when added to the model. Institutional type (dummy-coded; is_public) lost some statistical significance from 0.00329 to 0.00685, but is still statistically significant at the 0.001 level, as it was before.

Section 8: Final Model Diagnostics

8.1 Histogram of Standardized Residuals

hist(scale(model_final$residuals),
     main = "Histogram of Standardized Residuals",
     xlab = "Standardized Residuals",
     col = "lightblue",
     breaks = 20)

This histogram shows that the standardized residuals are normally distributed with slight right skewness.

8.2 Diagnostic Plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_final)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

8.3 Standardized Residuals

model_final$standardized.residuals <- rstandard(model_final)
model_final$large_residual <- abs(model_final$standardized.residuals) > 2

# How many potential outliers in the final model?
sum(model_final$large_residual)
## [1] 57

There are 57 potential outlier

8.4 Standardized Coefficients

lm(scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctpell) + scale(pell_cat) + scale(pctfloan) + scale(adm_rate) + scale(enrollment) + scale(control), data = college_data)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = scale(earn10yr) ~ scale(pctpell) + scale(pell_cat) + 
##     scale(pctfloan) + scale(adm_rate) + scale(enrollment) + scale(control), 
##     data = college_data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)          scale(pctpell)         scale(pell_cat)  
## -0.0000000000000004739  -0.4784615100763488504  -0.0653164360650257264  
##        scale(pctfloan)         scale(adm_rate)       scale(enrollment)  
##  0.0193096520575357644  -0.2161784133614776393   0.1483285421921900493  
##         scale(control)  
##  0.0769570056291549870

Percentage of students receiving Pell grants (pctpell), admission rate (adm_rate), and total enrollment (enrollment) have the largest effects here.

8.5 Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)

vif(model_final)
##         pctpell   pell_cat_high pell_cat_medium        pctfloan        adm_rate 
##        4.308811        2.748487        2.987405        1.811859        1.207389 
##      enrollment       is_public 
##        1.583720        1.694225

The VIFs are all below 5, which indicates no multicollinearity in the model.

8.6 Durbin-Watson Test

dwt(model_final)
##  lag Autocorrelation D-W Statistic p-value
##    1       0.1533699      1.693007       0
##  Alternative hypothesis: rho != 0

The Durbin-Watson test statistic is 1.69. Being close to 2, this indicates no autocorrelation in the residuals.

 

Section 9: Summary and Interpretation

9.1 Summary

The final regression model is statistically significant (p < 0.001) with about 39% (Adjusted R-squared = 0.386) of the variation in earnings after 10 years of graduation accounted for by the predictions of the model. Several variables significantly predict earnings:

  1. Percent of students receiving Pell Grants (pctpell) is a strong negative predictor (β = −349.4, p < .001). Colleges with higher proportions of Pell recipients tend to have lower average post-graduation earnings.

  2. Admission rate (adm_rate) is also negatively associated with earnings (β = −114.98, p < .001), suggesting that more selective institutions (lower admission rates) are associated with higher graduate earnings.

  3. Enrollment size (enrollment) shows a small but significant positive effect (β = 0.209, p < .001), meaning larger institutions tend to have slightly higher average earnings outcomes.

  4. Institution type (is_public) shows a noticeable difference, with public institutions showing significantly lower predicted earnings compared with private institutions (β = −1584.44, p = .0069).

 

9.2 Interpretation

Reflecting on my initial research question from Section 2, most of my expectations were supported by the final regression results. I anticipated that a higher percentage of Pell grant recipients (pctpell) at an institution would be linked to lower graduate earnings, and the analysis confirmed this association, likely reflecting broader socioeconomic challenges faced by these students. I did not anticipate total enrollment to have a positive relationship with median earnings 10 years after entry. The coefficient of 0.2516 suggests that for every 1000 enrolled students, the predicted median earnings are expected to increase by $2516. This runs contradictory to my hypothesis that small private schools would be associated with higher median earnings. This could be due to greater resources, opportunities, or alumni networks at larger institutions, which the results help reflect. Additionally, my hypothesis that more selective institutions (with lower admission rates) would have graduates with higher earnings held true, aligning with the idea that selectivity is associated with other valuable institutional qualities.What surprised me, however, was the relative strength of these relationships, especially how pronounced the association was for selectivity and enrollment. I also found it notable that after controlling for factors, these key predictors (pctpell, adm_rate, enrollment, and is_public) remained the most influential.

 

9.3 Limitations

Although this regression analysis offers valuable findings, it is important to recognize several key limitations:

  1. Correlation Does Not Imply Causation: Because the analysis relies on observational rather than experimental data, any associations identified cannot be taken as evidence of causality. The relationships observed between institutional characteristics and graduate earnings may be influenced by underlying factors not included in the model.

  2. Potential for Omitted Variables: There may be important factors affecting post-graduation earnings—such as academic rigor, local job markets, or student demographics—that were not measured or included. Excluding these variables could bias the estimated effects of the predictors used.

  3. Limits to Generalizability: The model is built on a specific dataset covering certain colleges and a particular cohort (10-year earnings). As such, its findings may not extend to all higher education contexts, different cohorts, or international settings.

  4. Multicollinearity and Specification: Even though VIFs suggest multicollinearity is not severe, some predictors may still be correlated, complicating interpretation of individual effects.

  5. Outliers and Model Robustness: While outliers were identified based on standardized residuals, they were not specifically adjusted for or examined further, which could impact the reliability of the results.

Altogether, these limitations mean the results should be interpreted with care. Further research with more comprehensive data, carefully selected variables, and alternative modeling approaches is needed to more fully understand the factors influencing graduate earnings.
